Political Systems We Should Totally Steal from SciFi and Fantasy

Not sure our current political systems are solving world problems fast enough? Here are 6 political systems from science fiction & fantasy for inspiration.

Political Systems and Time Management

Now, the first question you are probably asking is “Garland, you are all about time management. What do political systems have to do with time management?”

The political systems of the city, state/province, country and even the world drastically affect how we can work, travel, earn money, start businesses, or get an education. So they have a huge impact on time management, to say nothing of the amount of time people waste talking about and watching information about politics.

Political Systems and Science Fiction

I LOVE reading science fiction and fantasy! And there are some very intriguing societies and political systems that have been proposed by these very creative authors.

In my over 50 years of reading 5-10 books a week, here are my favorite political systems from scifi and fantasy that I think we should steal and implement here on Home Earth. Please note that these are my personal picks, not an exhaustive study of science fiction and fantasy literature and I am sure many other readers have their own favorites.

SPOILER ALERT! If you haven’t read some of these books yet, my discussion of them will create some spoilers for you.

#6: Federation – Star Trek

Star Trek was created in 1966 by Gene Roddenberry. Roddenberry and the writers who worked on the show created The United Federation of Planets, a forward-thinking political system that included many alien races along with humans. Some great things about the Federation include:

Racism largely absent with many alien races working together
Hunger and poverty largely eliminated (thank you replicators)
Money is not the central focus for people and organizations
Sentient rights are guaranteed

Not everything is perfect in the federation, of course, but the life of the average member of the Federation would be at a much higher level than in our current society and the less fortunate people would be better taken care of.

#5 Valdemar

Valdemar is a universe created by Mercedes Lackey. The first book, Arrow’s Flight, was published in 1987 and there are roughly 33 books in the series.

This is the only fantasy political system in the list, probably because most fantasies seem to be set in the past and often use feudal systems.

Monarchy Political Systems

Now, I am sure some of you are going. “Wait, doesn’t Valdemar have kings and queens? Isn’t is a monarchy. Aren’t monarchies some of the worst political systems?”

The answer is yes, but….

Valdemar is different.

Valdemar “Companions”

Valdemar Companions are spirit creatures that take the form of a white horse. Companions ONLY choose to be with people of good heart – these people become “Heralds.”
Heralds are a combination of messengers, peace keepers, diplomats, guards, soldiers and even deal with civil disputes.
ONLY royal heirs also chosen by Companions (heirs are also Heralds) are eligible to assume the throne.

So heirs are guaranteed to be people of good heart, brave, and with high moral integrity. If they aren’t they won’t be “chosen” by a Companion and can’t inherit the throne.

#4: Freehold of Grainne

Freehold is a book published by Michael Z Williamson, published in 2004.

In the book, a young woman Earth soldier is falsely framed for theft and flees Earth for Freehold. She finds Freehold very different than the ultra-authoritarian Earth she left.

The society in Freehold has these attributes:

Libertarian society
Big on individual rights
Small government, small taxation
True, free market society
Expects people to pull their own weight

Freehold isn’t perfect and not all people are good, but overall it is much better than the very corrupt Earth government that is trying to stamp it out.

#3: Chironians – Voyage to Yesteryear

Published in 1982 by James Hogan.

Fearing Earth may not survive, early in 21st century an automated probe is sent to a planet in Alpha Centauri system. Included is a bank of human knowledge, genetic material and robots to care for children created from genetic materials.
Probe reports back to Earth that they found a habitable planet and that the first generation of children have been raised successfully.
Later, Earth loses contact.

5 Generations Later

New technology lets Earth send manned colony ships. Each of the surviving power blocs North America, Europe and Asia send a ship with the North American ship due to arrive first.
The North American ship has an authoritarian regime, theocratic leaders, and a strong military presence and assume they will easily assume control of the colony when they arrive…

Upon Arrival

Upon arrival, the newcomers find a society of seemingly naïve people with no class system and no government. No money is in use – the factories easily turn out enough for everybody, so people just take and use what they need. No accumulation of wealth.

Chironians happily step aside if someone with more skill wants to do a task. They value competence and don’t let their egos get in the way.
The Chironians ignore people who are blowhards or phony – they only value what is real.

The Chironians are charming, and politely interested in the newcomers but think the newcomers are all a little uptight and cuckoo – especially those in charge.
The North American authoritarian regime is confused – who is in charge? And if they take over, will anyone pay attention to them?
But many of the passengers and military are finding the Chironian way of life to be very attractive…

 

#2: Hexies on Tharthee

This is a 2 book series, Saturnalia (1986) and A Lion on Tharthee (1987), published by Grant Callin.

The unique society outlined in the second book relies heavily on humor. People in the highest offices of the land are often elected after they sponsor or create “joke plays.” Humor is highly valued in politics, business and everyday life. And the humans are coming off as stuffed shirts in comparison and are in danger of being laughed off the planet.

Honorable Mentions

Here are a couple of honorable mentions I considered that didn’t make the final 6.

Beta Colony – Vorkosigan Series – Lois McMaster Bujold. Bujold is one of my favorite authors and creates great societies. The only reason that I didn’t include Beta Colony on this list is that the series mainly focused on the Barryar political system (more feudal and patriarchial) and the Beta Colony was one of multiple other societies that Barryar had to interact with. But Beta Colony looks like a great place to live with fantastic medical technology and their acceptance of unusual sexualities and even clones.
Rescue Party – Arthur C Clarke, 1946 – Rescue Party is only a short story so I didn’t include it in the final 6, but the group of aliens that comes to save Earth combines skills and aliens from many different cultures into a truly galactic civilization.
The Winner!

So here is Garland’s personal favorite pick for political systems from scifi and fantasy.

United Planets Council – First Salick War by Jean Johnson. Released July 2015. A 3-book series starting with The Terrans.

What do I love about this series and its politics?

Politicians serve their constituents first and take a daily oath of office to emphasize this
Poor approval levels among constituents can result in loss of political position
No direct lobbying of politicians allowed
Sufficient authority provided to accomplish tasks
Have telepaths – but held to high ethical standards and ethics are tested regularly
Not bullied by other human or alien political systems

The main character, a naval officer eventually elevated to Ambassador, has to interact with another human civilization political system as well as aliens and continues to act with integrity. A great read and a g

In almost every sci-fi story incorporating aliens, the Earth is shown as a single government entity rather than a multitude of nations as we are currently. Is this the ultimate governance structure of the future?
All related (33)
Sort
Recommended
Brian Blose
Software Developer, Novelist, Quora AddictAuthor has 1.8K answers and 5.2M answer views
 · 
10y

The single world government portrayed in science fiction is a hack to avoid writing about complex geopolitics. Would you really want to read hundreds of pages about the reactions of 196 countries to first contact with aliens? Even if you only include the major players, you would have the USA, China, Russia, the European Union (assuming they pool their resources to have more clout), and then state-funded terrorists and religious extremists who have particular opinions on the matter. A single government cuts away a lot of the politicking so that the story can focus on what everyone wants to r

Apparently Mark Will Suffice
Information enthusiastAuthor has 978 answers and 2.4M answer views

10y

There are a few reasons I can think of why authors often use this structure:

It is simply easier to tell the story of humans against - or in cooperation with - aliens if humans have a single world government.
The belief that alien contact and the (sadly likely impossible) ability to travel between stars in a timely fashion would have this effect on humanity - of realising that compared to these other species / planets, there is more that unites earth / humanity than divides us.
There has, over time, been a clear direction of travel to larger and larger entities, to use terminology borrowed fro
Continue Reading
Upvote

How come in most sci-fi movies and TV shows, the planets that have an intelligent species are united as one country, instead of being tens of hundreds of nations per planet?
Tomaž Vargazon
Practicing atheistAuthor has 11.2K answers and 456M answer views
 · 
2y

I think most are somewhat realistic in this regard, but not in the way you might think.

A TV show where a human cast (right) often meets and interacts with aliens (left)

Most shows don’t go into depth about how this alien society works or how that one does. They meet aliens and interact with them for a short while, then departs somewhere else. You do not see the entire intricacies of their society, just a few bits and pieces that are important to the plot.

Let’s flip this around and say IKS Klothos is sent on a mission of exploration and comes across present-day Earth. Aliens onboard have a missi

Who really runs the world?
Why is the UN closest to a world government?
A popular trope in science fiction is that an advanced spacefaring civilization always has a unified world government. Is this necessary? Can an Interstellar empire thrive without a world government?
If advanced aliens came to earth, knowing a lot about us, which earth government (or organisation) do you think they would choose to make contact with? Why?
What's up with the governments of the world?
Related
How come in most sci-fi movies and TV shows, the planets that have an intelligent species are united as one country, instead of being tens of hundreds of nations per planet?
Rodra Hascaryo
Studied Aerospace and Aeronautical Engineering at University of Illinois at Urbana-ChampaignAuthor has 7.5K answers and 109M answer views
 · 
2y

Well, it’s fiction.

One reason is that you want to keep the setting easily understandable. As the saying goes, “less is more”. You don’t need to understand the laws regulating grain trade to enjoy a political thriller. Too much details that are irrelevant to the plot is distracting, at best. With too many countries per planet, it will just be confusing.

Another reason is they want to give planets an identity. This is also why we often see those one-biome planets (like all of the planet is a giant desert).

As I said, most of the details are just not very important to the story so for all intents a

What's your most unhinged advice to combat the insane cost of living?
Lisa Dawson
Personal Finance Writer at The Penny Hoarder (2010–present)
 · 
Updated Wed

Let’s be real — the usual advice about skipping lattes or canceling Netflix is a joke. The cost of living has gone feral, and a $6 coffee isn’t the reason most of us are broke.

If you actually want to survive right now, you need tricks that feel a little off the rails — like slashing bills you didn’t even know were ripping you off, or getting paid for stuff you already waste time on anyway. It’s not glamorous, but it works. Here are the “unhinged” money moves that actually make a dent.

1. Cancel Your Car Insurance

You might not even realize it, but your car insurance company is probably overcharg

A single, planet-wide government is a common sci‑fi trope because it simplifies story logistics and dramatizes unified human response. But political, technological and social dynamics suggest several more plausible long-term governance architectures rather than an inevitable single global state. Below are the key drivers, plausible outcomes, and likely stability of each scenario.

Why the single-state trope is appealing (and misleading)

Narrative convenience: One polity equals one decision-maker for moral dilemmas, invasions, alliances and treaties.
Symbolic unity: Planetary government dramatizes
Continue Reading
Upvote
Related
If aliens landed on Earth, what would the government do?
Kamil Legowski
Studying Linguistics
 · 
4y

Which government is in question as there is not a single government in charge of Humanity. Diffrent places on Earth would react in different ways yet this question is more of how would humanity react. Most governments would do their best to protect the country and their leaders yet there would be not much that they could do except sending out their own armies to protect their land. Yet the invasion would definitely be far more cultural than the movies, due to the diverse possibilities of alien life there is no reasonable way for us to predict what would happen.

Confederation of Earth | Memory Gamma
content:
MEMORY GAMMA

in: Governments, Galactic superpowers, Universes
Confederation of Earth
SAVE
EDIT
Alternate timeline
(covers information from Confederation of Earth)

Confederation of Earth

Confederation flag standard (2401)

Founded:

2152

Status:

Active

Government:

Autocratic Oligarchy

Military:

Confederation Corps (Army based stellar fleet)

Homeworld:

Earth

Capital:

San Francisco

Species:

Human (soley)

Central Leaders:

Annika Hansen

Commanding Officers:

Jean-Luc Picard
Benjamin Sisko

Currency:

Confederacy Latinum credit

 

For an alternate timeline account of this Organization Click Here

The Confederation of Earth is an Authoritarian Oligarchic interstellar nation-state that is a successful amalgamation of several fascistic groups on Earth from the late 21st century. Its central goal is to create and racially homogenous and militarily secure Milky Way Galaxy for a now more socially introverted and Xenophobic Humanity, centrally by eradicating all alien species residing there.

It was created by manipulations in history from Q in 2024 to create another morality test for Jean-Luc Picard in 2401. However, the State's technical power grew so large, and Q, now a much older being in a deteriative state, accidentally transported other ships from Other universes and timelines along with Picard.

The Confederation ended up trying to make incursions into other realities through gaps in reality accidentally created by Q including creating a centuries long war with another Starfleet variant, the United Space Force.

These conflicts garnered the attention of the Federation, Imperial Federation, 2250s era Section 31, and several alien species, leading to near full scale war against the Confederation. This event was later named as the first "Multiversal Crisis" as the aftermath of this event would lead to several more similar events throughout the 25th century.

The Confederation is considered the "mirrored" (morally opposite) universal government of the United Space Force, as the Terran Empire is the moral opposite of Starfleet.

Contents
1 System of Government
2 Military Structure
3 History
4 External Links
System of Government

The Confederation, while centrally run by a Presidential based central pseudo-parliamentary republic structure. In reality, this is a political smokescreen for the actual ruling forces, which are a group of Earth and Offworld colony based super corporations that control and run all of Earth's technical and energy infrastructure, such as Yoyodyne Industries.

The publically shown government is headed by a central President, holding as Chairperson over a Council of "Magistrates". These Magistrates work similar to 20th century representative government. Each one speaking for the planetary regions or colonies of each regional sector.

However moreso in reality the government's task is to reinforce the xenophobic and intellectually backward tendencies of Human society to continue justifying the military's actions against all alien and outer universal factions. And to continue to give the Planets corporations massive profits through planetary annexation even though they live in a post scarcity technical society which these demands should be meaningless, but having a dominant hand over the planets resources does give them absolute political control.

Below that while it seems like the rest of Human society goes without, in reality due to having so many rich resources no one really goes hungry or homeless. However, Society within this universe has become so self serving that they are willing to participate in this corrupt system as they receive nearly as many perks as the elite (although not as elegant or high end) such as financial and resource bribes for smaller companies, gifted alien slave workforces, and guarantees on resource claims through piracy. Humanity gladly joins in on the corruption, as long as they are guaranteed something.

Military Structure
History

In 2024 years after the overthrow of Khan Noonien Singh and the end of the Eugenics Wars, another geneticist that was a survivor of the wars Adam Soong was in secret trying to create another project based on genetic superiority.

While in its infancy, synthetic engineered human technology or "Replicant" technology was attempted by several corporations, including the Tyrell Corporation in Los Angeles in 2019. Most projects were shut down due to hard laws against attempting to create another crisis of beings that believed they were superior to basic Humanity.

Adam, however, was attempting to mass produce such beings secretly, believing they could assist Humanity in the future. However, they were dying due to a genetic flaw. Each one living for only a few hours before complete organ failure. Adam was also in competition with Picard's 3xGreat Grandmother Renee for the development of an engineered airborne microorganism that could eat large amounts of artificial carbons in Earths atmosphere. Possibly reducing the climate crisis of general pollution and nuclear radiation leftover from the global conflicts of decades earlier.

Adam knew if Renee beat him out, he would be out of possible billions in genetic research.

It was because of this Q went to this particular time period and offered Adam a deal. Remove Renee from her discovery, and Q in return would give him the solution for the genetic defect and allow him to become the success Adam believed himself to be.

External Links
Confederation of Earth article at Memory Alpha, the canon Star Trek wiki.
Confederation of Earth article at Memory Beta, the non-canon Star Trek wiki.
